great-awakening.legacy: The First Great Awakening planted the seeds of American evangelical Christianity and contributed to the egalitarian spirit of the American Revolution (all people equal before God in their need of new birth). Edwards's theological defence of revival remains the most sophisticated treatment of the theology of awakening. Subsequent revivals — the Second Great Awakening, the 1857 revival, the Welsh Revival — all self-consciously drew on the Awakening's categories.

wesley-methodism.overview: John Wesley's Aldersgate experience on May 24, 1738 — feeling his heart 'strangely warmed' as Luther's preface to Romans was read — ignited a ministry that transformed Britain. Wesley preached over 40,000 sermons across fifty years, organising converts into 'class meetings' for accountability and spiritual growth. His theology of free grace (all can be saved), complete sanctification (the second blessing), and social holiness (the gospel applied to poverty, slavery, and prisons) shaped global Methodism.

key_people.name: John Wesley
key_people.role: Anglican priest; founder of Methodism; 1703–1791

key_people.name: Charles Wesley
key_people.role: John's brother; wrote over 6,000 hymns including 'And Can It Be' and 'O for a Thousand Tongues'

key_people.name: George Whitefield
key_people.role: Collaborated with Wesley in the early revival; later diverged on predestination

key_texts.note: Free grace for all — Wesley's foundation against Calvinist limited atonement
Complete sanctification — Wesley's distinctive doctrine of entire holiness

wesley-methodism.context: Wesley's open-air preaching reached coal miners and working-class people excluded from polite society. His class meetings were the first systematic lay discipleship programme since the early church — small groups meeting weekly for mutual accountability, Scripture, prayer, and economic mutual aid. The class meeting system became the template for the cell church movement two centuries later.
wesley-methodism.significance: Methodism introduced the combination of evangelical conversion (justification) with rigorous discipleship (sanctification) that became the template for much of evangelical Christianity. Wesley's insistence that personal salvation must issue in social transformation — opposing slavery, visiting prisons, feeding the poor — shaped the 19th-century evangelical social conscience.
wesley-methodism.legacy: United Methodism is the second-largest Protestant denomination in America. Holiness churches (Church of the Nazarene, Salvation Army), Pentecostalism, and the Charismatic movement all trace their roots to Wesley's theology of the Spirit's transforming work after conversion. His social vision influenced the abolitionist movement, prison reform, and child-labour laws.

william-carey.overview: William Carey, a Northamptonshire cobbler and Baptist pastor, sailed to India in 1793 with his family — the first Protestant missionary to India. He spent forty-one years without returning to England, learning Bengali, Sanskrit, and twenty-nine other languages, translating the complete Bible into six languages and portions into twenty-nine more, founding Serampore College, and catalysing a global Protestant missionary movement. His 1792 sermon used two headings: 'Expect great things from God; attempt great things for God.'

key_people.name: William Carey
key_people.role: 'Father of Modern Missions'; cobbler, linguist, botanist, missionary; 1761–1834

key_people.name: Joshua Marshman
key_people.role: Fellow Serampore missionary; Sanskrit scholar

key_people.name: William Ward
key_people.role: Printer; third member of the Serampore Trio

key_texts.note: 'How beautiful are the feet of him who brings good news' — missions as beautiful necessity
'Go and make disciples of all nations' — the Great Commission Carey took at face value

william-carey.context: Before Carey, Protestant Christianity had largely limited its missionary activity to Protestant Europe and colonial settlements. The widespread assumption was that the Great Commission applied only to the original apostles. Carey's Enquiry into the Obligations of Christians to Use Means for the Conversion of the Heathen (1792) systematically refuted this and provided statistical data on the unreached world.
william-carey.significance: Carey's life proved that ordinary people, with extraordinary faith and extraordinary work, could translate the Bible into dozens of languages, plant self-sustaining churches, and transform societies. His methods — learning the language, engaging the culture, establishing schools and colleges, producing vernacular literature — became the template for modern Protestant missions.
william-carey.legacy: The Protestant missionary movement Carey inspired sent missionaries across Africa, Asia, and the Pacific throughout the 19th century. Bible translation (the work of Wycliffe Bible Translators and similar organisations) continues his legacy. The modern missionary force of over 400,000 evangelical missionaries is the direct descendant of the movement Carey's Enquiry launched.

spurgeon.overview: Charles Haddon Spurgeon, converted at age sixteen, became pastor of the New Park Street Chapel in London at nineteen and built it into the Metropolitan Tabernacle — a congregation of six thousand, the largest in London. He preached to ten million people over his lifetime, produced 3,500 sermons (still the world's largest collection by a single author), founded Spurgeon's College, established sixty-six almshouses and charitable institutions, and battled the Down-Grade controversy — refusing theological compromise within the Baptist Union.

key_people.name: Charles Haddon Spurgeon
key_people.role: 'Prince of Preachers'; 1834–1892; Metropolitan Tabernacle

key_people.name: Thomas Olney
key_people.role: Deacon who helped manage Spurgeon's charitable institutions

key_texts.note: 'We preach Christ crucified' — Spurgeon's lifelong text
'Look unto me and be ye saved' — the text from which he was converted

spurgeon.context: Spurgeon preached before microphones, loudspeakers, or recording technology, yet tens of thousands heard him. His voice — trained by natural gifts and deliberate practice — could reach the far corners of the Royal Surrey Gardens Music Hall (12,000 capacity). His sermons, published in weekly penny pamphlets, circulated worldwide and were translated into dozens of languages.
spurgeon.significance: Spurgeon demonstrated that Reformed doctrines of grace and expository preaching of the whole Bible are not academic propositions but living fire. His theological robustness and pastoral warmth together refuted the assumption that Calvinism produces cold, intellectualist Christianity. His Down-Grade controversy — withdrawing from the Baptist Union over toleration of theological liberalism — anticipated the Fundamentalist-Modernist controversy by thirty years.
spurgeon.legacy: Spurgeon's sermons are still the most widely read Protestant sermons ever published. Spurgeon's College (London) trains Baptist ministers today. His influence on 20th-century preachers — D. Martyn Lloyd-Jones, John MacArthur, John Piper — is direct and acknowledged. He remains the most quoted preacher in the evangelical world.

azusa-street.overview: In April 1906, William Seymour, a one-eyed Black Holiness preacher, began prayer meetings in a private home in Los Angeles. After participants began speaking in tongues, the meetings moved to a former stable at 312 Azusa Street. For three years the revival ran day and night, with racial integration remarkable for the era — Black, White, and Latino worshippers together. Participants travelled from around the world and took the Pentecostal experience home.

key_people.name: William Seymour
key_people.role: Son of freed slaves; revival leader; 1870–1922

key_people.name: Charles Parham
key_people.role: Seymour's teacher; formulated the 'initial evidence' doctrine of tongues

key_people.name: Agnes Ozman
key_people.role: First person to speak in tongues at Parham's Bethel Bible School (1901) — Azusa's precursor

key_texts.note: 'All were filled with the Holy Spirit and began to speak in other tongues'
'I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh' — Peter quoted at Pentecost, Seymour quoted at Azusa

azusa-street.context: Azusa Street broke out in the aftermath of the San Francisco earthquake and during a period of intense prayer in global Holiness networks. Its racial integration was explicitly countercultural — Frank Bartleman wrote: 'The colour line was washed away in the blood.' The integrated worship was short-lived; by 1908 racial division had reasserted itself, a wound in Pentecostalism that has never fully healed.
azusa-street.significance: Azusa Street launched a movement that now numbers over 600 million — Pentecostals and Charismatics together form the fastest-growing segment of world Christianity. The Pentecostal insistence on the experiential reality of the Spirit's gifts, the gift of tongues as initial evidence of Spirit-baptism, and expectation of healing and prophecy defines a distinct trajectory of Christian life and worship.
azusa-street.legacy: Classical Pentecostalism (Assemblies of God, Church of God in Christ) directly descends from Azusa Street. The Charismatic Renewal in mainline denominations (1960s–70s) and the Third Wave of the Spirit (1980s–90s) are its grandchildren. Global Pentecostalism's explosive growth in Africa, Latin America, and Asia makes it the defining Christian movement of the 20th and 21st centuries.

fundamentalist-modernist.overview: The Fundamentalist-Modernist controversy of the 1920s–1930s divided American Protestantism between those who accepted historical-critical scholarship, evolution, and theological liberalism and those who insisted on the 'fundamentals' of historic Christian faith. The Fundamentals (1910–1915), a twelve-volume series distributed free across America, defined the essentials: biblical inerrancy, the virgin birth, substitutionary atonement, the bodily resurrection, and Christ's literal return. J. Gresham Machen's Christianity and Liberalism (1923) argued that Protestant liberalism was not Christianity at all but a different religion.

key_people.name: J. Gresham Machen
key_people.role: Princeton NT scholar; founded Westminster Theological Seminary (1929) and the Orthodox Presbyterian Church (1936)

key_people.name: Harry Emerson Fosdick
key_people.role: Liberal Baptist preacher whose 1922 sermon 'Shall the Fundamentalists Win?' provoked the controversy

key_people.name: William Jennings Bryan
key_people.role: Politician and Fundamentalist leader; argued for the prosecution at the Scopes Trial (1925)

key_texts.note: 'Contend earnestly for the faith once for all delivered to the saints' — the Fundamentalist watchword
A time is coming when people will not endure sound teaching

fundamentalist-modernist.context: The controversy took place as mainline denominations — Presbyterian, Methodist, Baptist — were systematically captured by theologically liberal leadership. Liberals controlled denominational seminaries, mission boards, and eventually publishing houses. Conservatives faced the choice of conforming, splitting, or forming parallel institutions. Many chose the latter — founding the institutions of neo-evangelical Christianity.
fundamentalist-modernist.significance: The controversy permanently bifurcated American Protestantism into mainline (liberal) and evangelical (conservative) streams, a divide that has only deepened. Machen's insight — that liberalism is not a purer Christianity but a different religion using Christian vocabulary — proved prophetic: mainline denominations have experienced decades of membership decline while evangelical churches have grown.
fundamentalist-modernist.legacy: The controversy produced Westminster Theological Seminary, the Orthodox Presbyterian Church, and set the theological trajectory of 20th-century American evangelicalism. Neo-evangelicalism (Billy Graham, Carl Henry, Christianity Today) was the post-WWII attempt to be both orthodox and academically engaged — a mediating position between raw Fundamentalism and liberal accommodation.

billy-graham.overview: William Franklin Graham Jr., a North Carolina farm boy converted in 1934, became the world's most recognisable evangelist following the 1949 Los Angeles Crusade that made him famous overnight. He preached to over 215 million people in 185 countries across six decades. He counselled every US President from Truman to Obama, co-founded Christianity Today magazine, and established the Billy Graham Evangelistic Association. His message never changed: God loves you; you are a sinner; Christ died for you; repent and believe.

key_people.name: Billy Graham
key_people.role: Evangelist; 1918–2018; reached more people with the gospel than any individual in history

key_people.name: Cliff Barrows
key_people.role: Music director and associate; led team worship across all Crusade years

key_people.name: George Beverly Shea
key_people.role: Soloist; 'I'd Rather Have Jesus' became the Crusade's signature

key_texts.note: Graham's most-cited verse: 'For God so loved the world'
'If you confess with your mouth... and believe in your heart' — the Crusade's response call

billy-graham.context: Graham's ministry coincided with the Cold War, the Civil Rights era, and the collapse of mainline Protestant Christianity. He desegregated his Crusades in 1953 — before Brown v. Board of Education — at significant cost. His ecumenical approach (directing converts to local churches of various denominations) was criticised by separatist Fundamentalists and remains debated among evangelicals.
billy-graham.significance: Graham embodied the neo-evangelical mediating position: orthodox theology, mass-culture engagement, and trans-denominational cooperation. His Lausanne Covenant (1974) defined global evangelical identity and commitment to world evangelisation. His moral integrity over sixty years — never travelling alone with a woman, keeping finances transparent, avoiding political entanglements — set standards for evangelistic ministries.
billy-graham.legacy: The Billy Graham Evangelistic Association continues training evangelists worldwide. The Lausanne Movement, which Graham catalysed, is the most significant global evangelical cooperation network. Hundreds of thousands of people who made decisions at Crusades went on to serve the church as pastors, missionaries, and lay leaders.

vatican-ii.overview: The Second Vatican Council (1962–1965), called by Pope John XXIII (aggiornamento — 'bringing up to date') and completed by Paul VI, was the most significant event in Roman Catholicism since Trent. It reformed the liturgy (Mass in the vernacular), affirmed religious liberty (Dignitatis Humanae), opened ecumenical dialogue with Protestants and Orthodox (Unitatis Redintegratio), acknowledged that 'many elements of sanctification and of truth are found outside its visible structure,' and launched Catholic engagement with modernity (Gaudium et Spes).

key_people.name: Pope John XXIII
key_people.role: Called the council; died before it completed; known for pastoral warmth

key_people.name: Pope Paul VI
key_people.role: Guided the council to completion; implemented its reforms

key_people.name: Cardinal Augustin Bea
key_people.role: Led ecumenical and Jewish dialogue — including the landmark Nostra Aetate declaration

key_texts.note: The unity of the body — the ecumenical vision driving dialogue with Protestants and Orthodox
'That they may all be one' — quoted in the council's ecumenism decree

vatican-ii.context: Vatican II took place during the Second World War's aftermath, the Cold War, decolonisation, and rapid global modernisation. The church faced both the Communist denial of religious liberty and the Western secular assumption of religion's irrelevance. Its 2,860 bishops from around the world represented a truly global church — the first such gathering in church history.
vatican-ii.significance: Vatican II produced a profound shift in Catholic self-understanding: from a fortress against modernity to a servant of the world; from a Latin-rite Western institution to a genuinely universal church. It ended the era of Tridentine Catholicism and opened dialogue with separated Christians for the first time since the Reformation.
vatican-ii.legacy: Vatican II's liturgical reforms, religious liberty declaration, and ecumenical openings transformed Catholic-Protestant relations. The Joint Declaration on Justification (1999, Lutheran and Catholic) and Catholic-Orthodox dialogues are its direct fruit. Its implementation remains contested within Catholicism — with traditionalists (attached to the pre-conciliar Mass) and progressives pulling in opposite directions.

global-south.overview: During the second half of the 20th century, the demographic centre of world Christianity shifted dramatically from the global North (Europe, North America) to the global South (sub-Saharan Africa, Latin America, and parts of Asia). In 1900, roughly 80% of Christians lived in Europe or North America. By 2000, a majority lived in the global South. By 2025, approximately two-thirds of the world's 2.6 billion Christians live in Africa, Asia, and Latin America — a transformation without precedent since the first century.

key_people.name: Philip Jenkins
key_people.role: Scholar who documented the shift in The Next Christendom (2002)

key_people.name: John Stott
key_people.role: British evangelical who championed a theology robust enough to address global South realities

key_people.name: Desmond Tutu
key_people.role: South African Anglican archbishop; theology of liberation rooted in Scripture

key_texts.note: 'A great multitude that no one could number, from every nation, tribe, people and language'
'All nations' — the Great Commission being fulfilled beyond the Western church's imagination

global-south.context: The growth of African Christianity in particular has been phenomenal: 10 million Christians in 1900, over 600 million by 2025. Much of this growth is Pentecostal and charismatic. Global South churches are typically theologically conservative — holding high views of Scripture, affirming orthodox Christology and morality — while Northern mainline churches have moved toward progressive positions.
global-south.significance: The theological implications are vast: the churches most engaged with mission, most literal in their reading of Scripture, and most willing to suffer for their faith are now the majority. Western Christianity's progressive drift is increasingly a minority position within world Christianity. The global South's theological conservatism is reshaping debates in Anglican, Methodist, and Catholic communions.
global-south.legacy: The Global Anglican Future Conference (GAFCON), formed in 2008 partly in response to North American Anglican departures from orthodox sexual ethics, represents the institutional emergence of global South theological influence. The 2022 Lambeth Conference was dominated by the question of whether the global South communion would walk away from the Canterbury centre — which millions did. The next chapter of Christianity is being written in Africa, Asia, and Latin America.
